title: P., 'Virgil and the Evictions,' Hermes 94 (

raw_text:
Vanderlinden, É., 'La dépossession de Virgile,' LEC 34 (1966) 35-8.
6.
Coleman, Robert, 'Tityrus and Meliboeus,' G&R 13 (1966) 79-97.
7.
Putnam, M. C. J., 'The Riddle ofDamoetas (Virgil Eel. 3, 104-105),' Mnemosyne 18 (1965) 150-4.
8.
Dahlmann, H., 'Zu Vergile siebentem Hirtengedicht,' Hermes 94 (1966) 218-32.
9.
Levi, P., 'The Dedication to Pollio in Virgil's
Eighth Eclogue,' Hermes 94
(1966) 73-79.
10.
Fantazzi, Charles, 'Virgilian Pastoral and Roman Love Poetry,' AJP 87 (1966)
171-91.
Segal (1) shows
greater concern with
the poetic
quality of the
poems than with the location of the farm, the tangled probably insoluble
problem of the confiscation and restoration
of the property.
His detailed analysis of Eclogues 1 and 9 as pendants to each other discovers,
inter alia, shadowy
elements
even
in Arcadia.
What
Panofsky has
called the 'vespertinal mixture of sadness and tranquillity' exists in
Vergil's repeated theme of exile from a peaceful, familiar world, and
constitutes Vergil's most personal contribution to poetry.
Fredericksmeyer (2) is not ready to accept Eclogue
1 as an outright eulogy of
Octavian.
He finds the poem ambivalent in its view of Octavian; his
past acts are assessed as productive of both good and evil, but he also
emerges as a saviour, a force for good, and a source of hope for the
future.
The familiar identification of Tityrus with Vergil, and the iuvenis
deus with Octavian, in Eclogue
1 is
challenged
by Grisart (3).
His
novel interpretation discovers a double objective: a masque involving
Vergil
and
the
youth Eros, combined with a dramatised
complaint
against expropriation of farms to compensate the veterans. Wilkinson
(4) examines Eclogues 1 and 9 in the light of pastoral conventions.
Allegorical interpretations are regularly embarrassed by the desire to
make all the details fit.
In fact Vergil's Eclogues are
Theocritean
pastorals with occasional outcroppings
of reality. Wilkinson's article,
though abbreviated, will serve as a corrective to H. J. Rose's literalmindedness in the criticism of the Bucolics, and as a useful corollary
to some of Vanderlinden's views (5).
Coleman's article (6) avoids the
biographical approach to the criticism of the Eclogues.
The intrusion
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:35 UTC
40
of the socio-political crisis is, in Coleman's view, original with Vergil.
By merging conventional Arcadia with this novel element, Vergil gave
pastoral poetry a new dimension.
Criticism
of
the Eclogues, with
Brooks Otis providing direction and challenge, has entered upon bright
new paths.
Putnam's (7) ingenious interpretation and solution
of the
riddle
of Damoetas deserves careful thought.
Dahlmann' s study (8) of
the seventh Eclogue marks some progress on the road to elucidation
but the argument is sometimes obscure and oblivious of important factors raised by Otis' study.
Levi's (9) concern with recent structural
studies of the Eclogues in their entirety brought him to review Eclogue
8, 6-13.
Textual and other factors, including the failure to cite Pollio
by name as the poem's recipient, induce Levi to accept only 11-13 as
genuine. Fantazzi's article (10) is a reduction of findings in his doctoral thesis, The Vergilian Version of Pastoral (Harvard, 1964).
Vergil revived the Theocritean dramatic form
and
Arcadian setting but
largely eliminated the exuberance and fervour of subjective love-poetry
in an idealized world of song.
The passion
and
the vicissitudes
of
elegiac love poetry were, in Vergil's view, anomalous in the pastoral
tranquillity of his new Arcadia.
Eclogue 10, to Cornelius Gallus, helps
one to demarcate the two poetic forms and moods and reminds one of
the pastoral break with passion.
11.
Buchheit, Vinzenz, 'Vergil in Sorge um Oktavian,' Rh. Mus. 109 (1966) 78-83.
12.
Abbe, E., The Plants of Virgil's Georgics.
Commentary and woodcuts. Ithaca,
1965.
13.
Morford, Mark P. O., 'Bruegel and the First Geòrgie,' G&R 13 (1966) 50-3.
14.
Volkmann, H., 'Zu Gallus -Inschrift auf dem Vatikanischen Obelisken,' Gymnasium 72 (1965) 328-30.
Highet, C., Franz Börner, 'Noch einmal die Gallus -Inschrift auf dem Vatikanischen Obelisken,' Gymnasium 73 (1966) 240.
Catullan
influence
on Vergil's
epic
has
received
considerable
notice in the past.
Buchheit (11) has discovered
fresh instances
of
influence in the Georgics.
Catullus 64
(35-42;
22-4;
394-9;
405-6)
clearly affected Vergil's craft and emotion in Georgics 1, 498ff.
Further study along these lines, with careful and imaginative attention to
the implications of the influence, and with due regard to metrics, word
order, etc. is greatly to be desired.
Miss Abbe's study of plants (12)
emerges from the Department of Botany at Cornell University.
Though
delightful to handle, and a masterful survey of plant- lore with careful
study of the survivals and parallels in modern Italian flora, the volume
must be expanded to include plants from the Eclogues and the Aeneid.
Breughel's landscape painting, 'The Fall
of Icarus' in Morford' s view
(12) is a marvelous combination of Ovidian detail and Vergilian spirit.
Product of Breughel's sojourn in Italy (1552-4) the painting, whether
consciously or not, provides a composite expression
of the underlying
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:35 UTC
41
theme of the Georgics.
The Vergilian
tradition
in Renaissance and
later landscape
and
mythological painting needs
attention.
Durer,
Breughel, Elsheimer, Claude and Poussin, Corot and Blake, all share
elements
of the Vergilian landscape, the myth of ideal rusticity.
Sir
Kenneth Clark has defined the Ideal or Vergilian landscape as 'essentially an evocation of the antique world, with all its fullness of life and
its confidence in the body, but also with some qualities which the ancient world lacked, some half-lights, some backward glances.'
(Landscape into Art.
London, 1949.
p. 55).
The Gallus inscription found
recently on the Vatican obelisk in St. Peter's Square continues to excite
discussion.
(14).
Gilbert Highet has challenged the earlier interpretations which located the obelisk in Egypt (Heliopolis) when Gallus added
the inscription.
Gallus' title, PRAEFECTUS FABRUM, in conjunction
with the phrase, FORUM JULIUM FECIT, might best attach to Gallus'
role as architect of the Forum Julium in Rome, completed by Augustus
(cf. Mon. Ane. 20, 12-4).
FECIT, rather than the normal PERFECIT,
may exemplify the vanity which finally brought Gallus to ruin and suicide.
It is also highly unlikely, in Highet' s view, that the colony
of
Forum Julium (mod. Fréjus) is implied, or that Gallus, as Governor of
Egypt, would refer to himself, in an Egyptian context, merely as Praefectus Fabrum, particularly when he felt emboldened to call himself
elsewhere, in the Philae inscription (ILS 8995) PRAEFECTUS ALEXANDREAE ET AEGYPTII PRIMUS.
Gallus' unquiet ghost keeps coming back to excite the archaeologist as
it once excited Vergil and the
Augustan elegists.
15.
Jones, J.W. Jr., 'Trojan Legend:
Who is Sinon?* CJ 61 (1965-6) 122-28.
16.
Lacroix, Leon, 'La légende de Philoctète en Italie méridionale,' Rev. Belge de
Phil. et d'Hist. 43 (1965) 5-21.
17.
Stocker, A. F. and A. H. Travis, Servianorum in Vergilii carmina commentariorum editionis Harvardianae volumen iii (Aeneid Ш-V).
Oxford, 1965.
18.
Tilley, Bertha, Vergil: Aeneid V (Palatine Classics), London, 1966.
19.
Smerdel, T., 'La scena tragica di Palinuro,' Živa Antika 2 (1966) 359-64.
20.
Segal, Charles P., 'Aeternum Per Saecula Nomen, The Golden Bough and the
Tragedy ofHistory, Part I,' Arion 4:4 (1965) 617-57.
21.
Avery, William T., 'The reluctant golden bough,' CJ 61 (1965-6) 269-72.
22.
Kienast, D., 'Rom und die Venus vom Eryx,' Hermes 93 (1965) 478-89.
23.
Holloway, W. Ross, 'The Tomb ofAugustus and the Princes ofTroy,'AJA 70
(1966) 171-3.
24.
Galinsky, G. Karl, 'Venus in a Relief ofthe Ara Pacis Augustae,' AJA 70 (1966)
223-43.
25.
Herrmann, L., 'Le 'puer' du sixième chant de l'Enéide,' Latomus
24 (1965)
421-2.
26.
Galinsky, G.Karl, 'The Hercules -Cacus Episode in Aeneid Vin,' AJP 87 (1966)
18-51.
27.
McGushin, P., 'Aeneas' Lasting City,' Latomus 24 (1965) 411-20.
28.
Reynen, H., 'Ewiger Frühling
und goldene Zeit.
Zum
Mythos des goldenen
Zeitalters bei Ovid und Vergil,' Gymnasium 72 (1965) 415-33.

Fantazzi, Charles, 'Virgilian Pastoral and Roman Love Poetry,' AJP 87 (1966) 171-91. Segal (1) shows greater concern with the poetic quality of the poems than with the location of the farm, the tangled probably insoluble problem of the confiscation and restoration of the property. His detailed analysis of Eclogues 1 and 9 as pendants to each other discovers, inter alia, shadowy elements even in Arcadia. What Panofsky has called the 'vespertinal mixture of sadness and tranquillity' exists in Vergil's repeated theme of exile from a peaceful, familiar world, and constitutes Vergil's most personal contribution to poetry. Fredericksmeyer (2) is not ready to accept Eclogue 1 as an outright eulogy of Octavian. He finds the poem ambivalent in its view of Octavian; his past acts are assessed as productive of both good and evil, but he also emerges as a saviour, a force for good, and a source of hope for the future. The familiar identification of Tityrus with Vergil, and the iuvenis deus with Octavian, in Eclogue 1 is challenged by Grisart (3). His novel interpretation discovers a double objective: a masque involving Vergil and the youth Eros, combined with a dramatised complaint against expropriation of farms to compensate the veterans. Wilkinson (4) examines Eclogues 1 and 9 in the light of pastoral conventions. Allegorical interpretations are regularly embarrassed by the desire to make all the details fit. In fact Vergil's Eclogues are Theocritean pastorals with occasional outcroppings of reality. Wilkinson's article, though abbreviated, will serve as a corrective to H. J. Rose's literalmindedness in the criticism of the Bucolics, and as a useful corollary to some of Vanderlinden's views (5). Coleman's article (6) avoids the biographical approach to the criticism of the Eclogues. Herrmann, L., 'Le 'puer' du sixième chant de l'Enéide,' Latomus 24 (1965) 421-2. 26. Galinsky, G.Karl, 'The Hercules -Cacus Episode in Aeneid Vin,' AJP 87 (1966) 18-51. 27. McGushin, P., 'Aeneas' Lasting City,' Latomus 24 (1965) 411-20. 28. Reynen, H., 'Ewiger Frühling und goldene Zeit. raw_text:
Williams, R. D., 'Aeneid 11.
400-409/ CP 61 (1966) 184-6.
30.
Wittenburg, Sister Mary Ste. Therese, S.N.D., 'Vergil's Camilla/ CB 42 (1966)
69-71.
31.
Owens, Sister M. Lilliana, S. L., 'Comprehensiveness and Purpose intheAeneis,' CB 42 (1966) 87-89.
Sinon, monster
of
insinuating duplicity, in Jones' view (15) was
Vergil's creation
(Aen. 2, 57-198).
But Homer's Odyssey 8, 494, implies that a Sinon- like character existed in the earliest known stages of
the Trojan legend, with a role as climactic as the Wooden Horse of
Epeios.
Vergil's model was almost certainly Odysseus as delineated
in Euripides' (now lost) Philoctetes. (The pendant to the Sinon incident,
the Achaemenides episode, finds its inspiration in the same play). Lacroix' s (16) study of the Philoctetes (and Epeios)
legends
in
Magna
Graecia provides useful expansion of Helenus' advice to Aeneas (Aen.
3, 402) that Philoctetes had established himself at Petelia in South Italy
(cf. Strabo 6, 254).
The indispensable Servius commentary (17), under
new
editorial direction, provides authentic and enlightening material
(and asides) for students
of Vergil.
Bertha Tilly (18) widely respected
for her useful topographical studies of places in the Aeneid, provides a
compact and thoughtful school edition of Aeneid V.
On the whole, R. D.
Williams' recent text and commentary (Oxford, 1962) provides a better
balanced and more scholarly commentary, but for the final year of High
School and the first year of College Bertha Tilly's text will serve admirably.
Palinurus' unquiet grave on 'A shelfy Coast,/ long infamous
for Ships, and Sailors lost;/ and white with Bones' in Dryden's version,
has a fascination for readers and critics
of Vergil.
Smerdel (19) has
discovered a basically tragic structure and design to the episode, and a
marked divergence by Vergil
from
the
undoubted
model,
Elpenor' s
story, in Homer's Odyssey 10, 552-60. Segal's (20) study is even more
suggestive, and actually explores the implications of Palinurus' demise
in a larger framework.
Concerned initially with the complex symbolism of the Golden Bough, Segal has endeavoured to penetrate the mystery attaching to the talisman, its
relationship
to
Misenus, and
the
tragic and other-worldly associations with both Daedalus and Palinurus.
The second part of this study is eagerly awaited.
Avery (21), less concerned than some scholars with the use of cunctantem when Aeneas'
plucks the Golden Bough (Aenè 6, 211; contra 6, 143-8), pleads for what
should seem obvious to more critics.
The Aeneid is after all a poem,
a work of art, not a laboratory report, and consequently requires less
stringent analysis.
Cult and monument have received their share of
attention
recently.
Kienast's study (22)
of the
Venus
Erycina
cult,
combined with new finds at Segesta, further enlarges the implications
of the death and funeral rites of a heroized Anchises at Drepanum. The
tumular burials
of Polydorus, Misenum, and Caieta have often been
aligned with the Mausoleum of Augustus in the Campus Martius. Holloway
(23)
instances a Homeric parallel
in
the projected tumulus
of
Hector in the Troad, to be set in full view
of the ships passing through
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:35 UTC
43
the Dardanelles.
He rejects the Etruscan mounded tombs as the inspiration for the Augustan family tomb, and favours the 'heroic' versions adopted by the epic poets, including Vergil, as the propagandaladen
models
for a 'Trojan'
Augustus.
Scholars
have
frequently
detected structural and illustrative parallels between the Aeneid and
the Augustan Ara Pads. Henning Malmstrom' s Ara Pads and Vergil's
Aeneid:
A Comparative Study (Malmö, 1963) even suggested that the
march-past of Roman heroes-to- come in Anchises'
review
(Aen. 6)
found a direct reflection, even identification, in the historical
relief
processions on the Augustan monument.
Galinsky's article (24) finds
reason to identify the Tellus or Italia panel with Venus, called Aeneadum genetrix by Lucretius, and
in Italy customarily
identified with
natural and garden fertility.
He provides several convincing illustrations from the text of the Aeneid to support his claim.
Herrmann (25)
find additional reasons to identify the 'puer' of Eclogue 4 ('Messianic')
with the short-lived Marcellus in Aeneid 6.
Galinsky (26) discovers
three functions in the Hercules- Cacus episode in Aeneid 8:
to echo and
foreshadow various motifs of other books and so to help unify the epic;
by parallels of imagery, language, symbolism and occasionally theme
to link the 'volcanic' episode to Aeneid 2 and to Aeneid 12, for Hercules
and Cacus surely foreshadow Aeneas and Turnus; and finally, by reason
of the relationship between Books 6 and 8, closer than that between 2
and 4, to aid and abet the structure of the Aeneid as a triology.
The
Golden Age has received careful study from two scholars, an Australian
(27) and a German (28).
The Golden Age, when man lived on the fruits
of the earth, in peace and piety, and with primitive simplicity, was a
preoccupying theme with the Augustan poets.
Reynen (28) shows how
Ovid and Vergil viewed this ideal paradise, lost and regained with the
advent of Augustus.
McGushin (27) shows that the ideal of wealth, and
repeated emphasis on gold, feature in the description of all the cities
encountered by Aeneas save one, the simple
settlement of Arcadian
Evander at Pallanteum.
Aeneas gradually realises that a material city
(moenia) and material power are not enough.
Simplicity, endurance,
and
the
Saturnian mores are
more
important
and durable than the
splendour, comfort, and enervating atmosphere of wealth and prosperity.
Williams (29) offers a new
interpretation
of
Turnus'
reply
to
Drances.
He excludes Aen. 11, 404 (cf. Aen. 2, 197) as an inappropriate remark
for Turnus;
but he regards the Aufidus allusion as extremely pertinent to Turnus, for his homeland, before Ardea, was the
territory of Daunus where the obstreperous Aufidus (cf. Horace, Odes
3, 30, 10) was not likely to run backwards.
32.
Clausen, W. V., F. R. D. Goodyear, E. J. Kenney, J.A. Richmond, Appendix
Vergiliana.
Oxford, 1966.
(Oxford Classical Text).
33.
Giorgio Brugnoli - Riccardo Scarcia, 'Osservazioni sulla 'Vita Probiana* di
Virgilio,' Studi Urbinati 39 (1965) 18-46.
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:35 UTC
_44
A quartet of editors (32) has
produced a new edition to replace
Ellis' Appendix Vergiliana (1907).
The styles of the individual poets
(there is no single authorship) are carefully reviewed. Aetna is included; the Vitae Vergilianae are to appear in a separate volume.
The
preface and notes are in Latin.
The
Vita Probiana (33) has been subjected to a careful study with particular attention to Pietole- Andes, the
poeťs birthplace, the mother's name, Magia Polla, etc.
34.
Conrad, C. W., 'Traditional Patterns ofWord-Order in Latin Epic from Ennius
to Vergil,' HSCP 69 (1965) 195-258.
35.
Pearce, T. E. V., 'The Enclosing Word Order in the Latin Hexameter, I,' CQ
16 (1966) 140-71.
36.
Duckworth, George E., 'Variety and Repetition in Vergil's Hexameters' ТАРА
95 (1964) 9-65.
37.
Perret, Jacques, Virgile. Paris, 1965.
Second edition.
38.
Segal, Charles P., rev. Brooks Otis, Virgil, a Study in Civilized Poetry
(Oxford, 1963) Arion 4:1 (1965) 126-49.
39.
Duckworth, George E., rev. Brooks Otis, Virgil, a Study in Civilized Poetry
(Oxford, 1963), CP 60 (1965) 409-20.
40.
Bruère, Richard T., rev. Mario A. Di Cesare, Vidays
"Christiad" and Vergilian Epic (New York, 1964), CP 61 (1966) 21-43.
41.
Copley, F. O., Vergil, Aeneid:
a verse translation, with an introduction by
Brooks Otis.
Indianapolis, 1965.
42.
Reinhold, Meyer, Barrow's Simplified Approach toVergil: Eclogues, Georgics,
Aeneid.
New York, 1966.
(Barron's Educational Series).
The
patterns
of word order and the distribution of dactyls
and
spondees in the hexameter line have become
increasingly
important
factors in the study of structure in the works
of Vergil, particularly in
the Aeneid.
Conrad (34) and Pearce (35) follow comparable lines of
research.
Conrad's survey is more historical.
He shows that there
was a tradition of word- order in Latin hexameter poetry going back to
Homer.
He also shows how each of the patterns
of separation of substantive and attribute is formed, and what effects it makes.
Vergil,
among the Roman poets, proved most loyal to Homer; like Horace he
was ready to appropriate a tradition, to master the inherited forms and
express his own national and personal character through them.
Duckworth's researches (35), amplified in this volume
of Vergilius, deal
with metrical procedures, with the frequencies of sixteen metrical patterns, and with the frequencies
of repeated, opposite, and reverse patterns in adjacent lines.
His major finds are e.g., the Catullan nature
of Eclogue 4, and the surprising manner in which Aeneid 10-12 differ
metrically from the rest of the epic.
The lack of metrical variety in
the last three books, and the use of adjacent patterns which are vastly
different
from those found elsewhere in Vergil's
poetry suggest
to
Duckworth that although by 19 B.C. the necessary metrical revisions
had progressed through Aeneid 9, the curious and unusual feature
of a
metrical
variety
in
the
remaining books
are the mark of Vergil's
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:35 UTC
45
failure to revise them before his death.
Such a conclusion has farreaching importance
for students
of epic
composition.
The Perret
study (37) has been revised and eight new plates added.
The literary
criticism, particularly of the Eclogues, marks a sensitive, penetrating
incursion into the old hunting ground.
The Eclogues still constitute an
important gap in Vergilian scholarship, as does the post- Renaissance
tradition
of Vergil.
Segal's
(38)
and Duckworth's
(39)
reviews
of
Brooks Otis' Virgil: A Study in Civilized Poetry are comprehensive
and instructive. Bruère's (40) review article of de Cesare's study of
the Christiad by Vida is masterly and enlightening. Copley's new verse
translation of the Aeneid (41) is a line-for-line version based on the
English iambic pentameter, but with free substitution
of anapaestic,
dactylic, and even trochaic feet for iambics.
Not words but ideas are
the translator's aim in a version that has aimed to preserve dignity,
clarity, and immediate comprehensibility in a compressed
style.
In
reaction probably
to the C. Day Lewis version, Copley claims
that
'every effort has been made to avoid the witty and mannered, the outré
and archaic, except where a truly ancient or fantastic idea seemed to
demand such expression.' Otis' introduction is relevant and significant.
Three maps and a glossary assist the reader.
A. G. McKay
McMaster University,
September, 1966.
Black- Figured Hydria, showing Aeneas bearing Anchises from Troy.
Courtesy Montreal Museum ofFine Arts.

Holloway (23) instances a Homeric parallel in the projected tumulus of Hector in the Troad, to be set in full view of the ships passing through 77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:35 UTC 43 the Dardanelles. He rejects the Etruscan mounded tombs as the inspiration for the Augustan family tomb, and favours the 'heroic' versions adopted by the epic poets, including Vergil, as the propagandaladen models for a 'Trojan' Augustus. Scholars have frequently detected structural and illustrative parallels between the Aeneid and the Augustan Ara Pads. Henning Malmstrom' s Ara Pads and Vergil's Aeneid: A Comparative Study (Malmö, 1963) even suggested that the march-past of Roman heroes-to- come in Anchises' review (Aen. 6) found a direct reflection, even identification, in the historical relief processions on the Augustan monument. Galinsky's article (24) finds reason to identify the Tellus or Italia panel with Venus, called Aeneadum genetrix by Lucretius, and in Italy customarily identified with natural and garden fertility. He provides several convincing illustrations from the text of the Aeneid to support his claim. Herrmann (25) find additional reasons to identify the 'puer' of Eclogue 4 ('Messianic') with the short-lived Marcellus in Aeneid 6. Galinsky (26) discovers three functions in the Hercules- Cacus episode in Aeneid 8: to echo and foreshadow various motifs of other books and so to help unify the epic; by parallels of imagery, language, symbolism and occasionally theme to link the 'volcanic' episode to Aeneid 2 and to Aeneid 12, for Hercules and Cacus surely foreshadow Aeneas and Turnus; and finally, by reason of the relationship between Books 6 and 8, closer than that between 2 and 4, to aid and abet the structure of the Aeneid as a triology. The Golden Age has received careful study from two scholars, an Australian (27) and a German (28). The Golden Age, when man lived on the fruits of the earth, in peace and piety, and with primitive simplicity, was a preoccupying theme with the Augustan poets. Reynen (28) shows how Ovid and Vergil viewed this ideal paradise, lost and regained with the advent of Augustus. McGushin (27) shows that the ideal of wealth, and repeated emphasis on gold, feature in the description of all the cities encountered by Aeneas save one, the simple settlement of Arcadian Evander at Pallanteum. Aeneas gradually realises that a material city (moenia) and material power are not enough. Simplicity, endurance, and the Saturnian mores are more important and durable than the splendour, comfort, and enervating atmosphere of wealth and prosperity. Williams (29) offers a new interpretation of Turnus' reply to Drances. He excludes Aen. 11, 404 (cf. Aen. 2, 197) as an inappropriate remark for Turnus; but he regards the Aufidus allusion as extremely pertinent to Turnus, for his homeland, before Ardea, was the territory of Daunus where the obstreperous Aufidus (cf. The patterns of word order and the distribution of dactyls and spondees in the hexameter line have become increasingly important factors in the study of structure in the works of Vergil, particularly in the Aeneid. Conrad (34) and Pearce (35) follow comparable lines of research. Conrad's survey is more historical. He shows that there was a tradition of word- order in Latin hexameter poetry going back to Homer. He also shows how each of the patterns of separation of substantive and attribute is formed, and what effects it makes. Vergil, among the Roman poets, proved most loyal to Homer; like Horace he was ready to appropriate a tradition, to master the inherited forms and express his own national and personal character through them. Duckworth's researches (35), amplified in this volume of Vergilius, deal with metrical procedures, with the frequencies of sixteen metrical patterns, and with the frequencies of repeated, opposite, and reverse patterns in adjacent lines. His major finds are e.g., the Catullan nature of Eclogue 4, and the surprising manner in which Aeneid 10-12 differ metrically from the rest of the epic. The lack of metrical variety in the last three books, and the use of adjacent patterns which are vastly different from those found elsewhere in Vergil's poetry suggest to Duckworth that although by 19 B.C. the necessary metrical revisions had progressed through Aeneid 9, the curious and unusual feature of a metrical variety in the remaining books are the mark of Vergil's 77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:35 UTC 45 failure to revise them before his death. Such a conclusion has farreaching importance for students of epic composition. The Perret study (37) has been revised and eight new plates added. The literary criticism, particularly of the Eclogues, marks a sensitive, penetrating incursion into the old hunting ground. The Eclogues still constitute an important gap in Vergilian scholarship, as does the post- Renaissance tradition of Vergil. Segal's (38) and Duckworth's (39) reviews of Brooks Otis' Virgil: A Study in Civilized Poetry are comprehensive and instructive. Bruère's (40) review article of de Cesare's study of the Christiad by Vida is masterly and enlightening. Copley's new verse translation of the Aeneid (41) is a line-for-line version based on the English iambic pentameter, but with free substitution of anapaestic, dactylic, and even trochaic feet for iambics. Not words but ideas are the translator's aim in a version that has aimed to preserve dignity, clarity, and immediate comprehensibility in a compressed style. In reaction probably to the C. Day Lewis version, Copley claims that 'every effort has been made to avoid the witty and mannered, the outré and archaic, except where a truly ancient or fantastic idea seemed to demand such expression.' Otis' introduction is relevant and significant. Three maps and a glossary assist the reader. A. G. McKay McMaster University, September, 1966.
